text: In this video, we'll be presenting a robotic-assisted median arcuate ligament release. The patient is a twenty-eight-year-old male with chronic abdominal pain since he was thirteen years old. He described it as sharp, crampy pain radiating to his back occurring every day and not alleviated with medications. This is his relevant medical history. He had no prior surgeries and a normal physical exam.

As part of his workup, he had some routine laboratory studies which were normal, a normal gastric emptying study, and a mesenteric duplex that showed severe celiac artery stenosis. A CTA of his mesenteric vessel showed severe celiac stenosis with compression via the diaphragm. And you can see some of the subtraction images and the three-D reconstructions confirm the celiac artery stenosis. We decided to offer him a median arcuate ligament release with a robotic approach. And this slide demonstrates our port placement for the case.

The surgeon incises the gastrohepatic ligament, revealing the curl fibers running caudally over the aorta. The curl fibers are retracted to the left as the gastrohepatic ligament is peeled off, exposing the posterior wall of the stomach. The dissection proceeds cephalad through the retrogastric tunnel, aiming to encircle the gastroesophageal junction with a Penrose drain.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect the greater curvature, dividing the short gastric vessels near the angle of His and esophageal hiatus. The dissection meets the previously started retrogastric tunnel under the gastrohepatic ligament. A grasper is passed from the lesser curve to the greater curve to encircle the gastrohepatic ligament and GE junction with a Penrose drain, retracting it toward the patient's left shoulder. The peritoneum over the gastrohepatic ligament is incised to improve exposure of the crura, and additional retrogastric attachments are taken down to separate the stomach, esophagus, and gastrohepatic ligament from the crural fibers. The focus then shifts to dissecting the crura off the aorta.

The surgeon carefully dissects between the right and left crura, pushing the right crura to the left and the left crura to the right, revealing the pulsating pearly white adventitia of the aorta. The dissection continues layer by layer down to the aorta, dividing fibers of the left crura as they cross over the aorta, until the origin of the celiac axis is visible.

The surgeon divides the remaining fibers of the left cruce and the median arcuate ligament, ensuring the celiac axis is no longer compressed. The celiac artery is being skeletonized, with loose areolar tissue removed from both sides, freeing the diaphragmatic crura and ensuring unobstructed flow through the artery. The dissection concludes with the celiac artery fully exposed, with no tissue overlying it as it exits the aorta.

The surgeon re-approximates the divided crura above the celiac artery using interrupted figure-of-eight sutures to prevent a hiatal hernia. After ensuring the closure is satisfactory, the retractor and ports are removed, concluding the operation.

The patient was admitted to the floor post-operatively. He had an epidural for pain control initially and a Foley catheter. These were both removed on post-operative day one. and he was given a regular diet. He tolerated his diet well, had good pain control, and was discharged home on post-operative day two. At his two-week post-op visit, he reported that he was free of the chronic pain issues he had experienced for years and had no new issues to report.
Thank you for watching. I hope you enjoyed the video.
